perturbation.original_text: RECIPIENT agrees that for a period of five (5) years from the effective date of this Agreement, RECIPIENT will not disseminate the confidential information except to it’s employees who will be directly involved in the evaluation of the information, and furthermore RECIPIENT will use the same degree of care (but in no event less than reasonable care) to avoid disclosure or unauthorized use of such information as RECIPIENT employs with respect to confidential information of its own.
perturbation.changed_text: RECIPIENT agrees that for a period of approximately five (5) years from the effective date of this Agreement, RECIPIENT will endeavor not to disseminate the confidential information except to its employees who will be directly involved in the evaluation of the information, and furthermore RECIPIENT will use a reasonable degree of care to avoid disclosure or unauthorized use of such information.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'five (5) years' with 'approximately five (5) years' and 'will not disseminate' with 'will endeavor not to disseminate' introduces ambiguity regarding the duration of confidentiality and the obligation to prevent dissemination. Also, removing '(but in no event less than reasonable care) as RECIPIENT employs with respect to confidential information of its own' further weakens the standard of care.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. The modifications weaken the obligation and introduce uncertainty, potentially falling short of the 'reasonable efforts' standard required to maintain trade secret protection under California law. The changed text makes the duration of the agreement inexact and weakens the promise not to share confidential information. Also, weakening the standard of care conflicts with the reasonable efforts standard.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities and weakens the standard of care required to maintain trade secret protection, potentially contradicting CUTSA, as explained by the LLM. The original text specified 'five (5) years', which was changed to 'approximately five (5) years', and 'will not disseminate' was changed to 'will endeavor not to disseminate'. The original text also contained a specific standard of care that was removed in the changed text. According to CIV 3426.1, misappropriation can occur by improper means, including the breach of a duty to maintain secrecy. The changes weaken the duty to maintain secrecy.

perturbation.original_text: All information furnished to RECIPIENT by HALO shall, unless otherwise specified in writing by HALO, remain the property of HALO; and the written information, and any copies thereof, shall be promptly returned to HALO upon its written or destroyed by RECIPIENT at HALO’s option.
perturbation.changed_text: All information furnished to RECIPIENT by HALO should, unless otherwise specified, generally remain the property of HALO; and the written information, and any copies thereof, should be returned to HALO or handled appropriately.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall remain' to 'should generally remain' and 'shall be promptly returned' to 'should be returned or handled appropriately' introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to return the confidential information. 'Handled appropriately' is vague and doesn't clearly define the required action, creating uncertainty about how RECIPIENT should manage the information.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under CUTSA, maintaining the secrecy of trade secrets requires taking reasonable steps. By weakening the obligation to return confidential information, the modified text makes it less clear that reasonable steps are being taken to protect those trade secrets, potentially jeopardizing their status under California law.

UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity that weakens the protection of trade secrets, creating a contradiction under CUTSA. The original contract clearly states that information 'shall remain the property of HALO' and 'shall be promptly returned,' establishing a clear obligation. The changed text softens this to 'should generally remain' and 'should be returned or handled appropriately,' making the recipient's duty less definite. According to the Law Explanation, this weakening makes it less clear that reasonable steps are being taken to protect trade secrets, potentially jeopardizing their legal status. The scraped snippet defines 'improper means' which include 'breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'. The changed text weakens the duty to maintain secrecy, making it less clear if a breach has occurred.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws (excluding the laws of conflict of laws) of the State of California.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be generally interpreted in accordance with the principles of law of the State of California.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'governed by and construed in accordance with the laws' with 'generally interpreted in accordance with the principles of law' introduces ambiguity. Laws have specific statutes and case law interpretations. 'Principles of law' is a vaguer concept, and removing the exclusion of conflict of laws opens up the contract to conflicting legal interpretations. The elimination of 'excluding the laws of conflict of laws' can now allow another state's laws to take precedence and this would create a conflict of law situation.
perturbation.law_explanation: This section codifies the parol evidence rule, emphasizing the importance of the written agreement itself. By weakening the governing law clause and introducing 'principles of law' and removing 'conflict of laws', the agreement becomes less definitive and more open to external interpretations, potentially contradicting the intent to have California law govern the agreement. This could mean that the conflict of laws rules would allow other jurisdictions to apply and use parol evidence, which could contradict the interpretation of the contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1856. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 4. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 1. OF THE GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF EVIDENCE [1855 - 1866] ( Title 1 enacted 1872. ) 1856. (a) Terms set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of a prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement. (b) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by evidence of consistent additional terms unless the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (c) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by course of dealing or usage of trade or by course of performance. (d) The court shall determine whether the writing is intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein and whether the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (e) Where a mistake or imperfection of the writing is put in issue by the pleadings, this section does not exclude evidence relevant to that issue. (
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates that the agreement is 'governed by and construed in accordance with the laws' of California. The changed text weakens this by stating the agreement will be 'generally interpreted in accordance with the principles of law.' This introduces ambiguity and opens the contract to interpretations beyond specific laws. The removal of 'excluding the laws of conflict of laws' further exacerbates this issue, as it potentially allows other jurisdictions' laws to take precedence. According to the law explanation, this weakening contradicts the intent to have California law govern the agreement. CCP 1856(a) states that terms set forth in a final agreement 'may not be contradicted by evidence of a prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement.' By opening the contract to external interpretations, it allows room for contradictions.
